coredata.dc:title: Experimental study of a novel nanofluid cooling system based on fly ash and serpentine channels for modular lithium-ion battery thermal control
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wankhede, Sagar
Pingale, Ajay
Patil, Shweta
Shahane, Kaustubh
coredata.dc:description: In an effort to combat global warming, several countries have pledged to achieve carbon neutrality by 2050. One of the most crucial and significant responsibilities in achieving carbon neutrality is transportation. The present generation of combustion-engine automobiles may be replaced by energy-efficient pure electric vehicles (EVs) and hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs) powered by green energy in response to global energy and environmental issues. More batteries are needed for EVs than ever before. According to this viewpoint, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are exceptional energy storage devices that have gained widespread usage because of their many outstanding advantages. EVs usually employ the battery thermal management system (BTMS) since LIBs are sensitive to temperature. With the aid of a BTMS, an electric car's lithium-ion battery may operate between 15 and 35°C. The efficiency of LIBs is also impacted by the internal heat generated while charging and discharging. Thus, a battery heat control system is needed. Liquid-cooled systems can effectively regulate the LIB pack's temperature in both charge and discharge situations. The utilization of fly ash nanoparticles distributed in water-as a coolant in indirect liquid cooling systems is the primary objective of the current work. The fly ash nanoparticle concentration and the BTMS capacity to remove heat were found to be clearly correlated in the experimental findings of the fly ash-based nanofluid (NF). In comparison to the base fluid, the fly ash water-based NF was able to remove around 10.65% more heat at a concentration of 0.1% by volume of nanoparticles, and approximately 11.30% more heat at a concentration of 0.3% and 13.04% more heat at a concentration of 1% volume fraction of fly ash-based nanoparticles. Increased thermal conductivity and better convective heat transfer properties of the NF with a greater fly ash concentration are responsible for this improvement in thermal performance. Comparing these nanofluids to traditional coolants, experiments have shown that they can lower thermal resistance and increase heat transfer coefficients. According to the study's findings, fly ash-based NF perform better at cooling than traditional coolants like ethylene glycol and water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery cooling
Fly ash
Nanofluids
Nanoparticles
Electric Vehicle

head.ce:title: Experimental study of a novel nanofluid cooling system based on fly ash and serpentine channels for modular lithium-ion battery thermal control

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Pimpri Chinchwad College of Engineering, Pune, 411044, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Pimpri Chinchwad College of Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Pimpri Chinchwad College of Engineering, Pune, 411044, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In an effort to combat global warming, several countries have pledged to achieve carbon neutrality by 2050. One of the most crucial and significant responsibilities in achieving carbon neutrality is transportation. The present generation of combustion-engine automobiles may be replaced by energy-efficient pure electric vehicles (EVs) and hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs) powered by green energy in response to global energy and environmental issues. More batteries are needed for EVs than ever before. According to this viewpoint, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are exceptional energy storage devices that have gained widespread usage because of their many outstanding advantages. EVs usually employ the battery thermal management system (BTMS) since LIBs are sensitive to temperature. With the aid of a BTMS, an electric car's lithium-ion battery may operate between 15 and 35°C. The efficiency of LIBs is also impacted by the internal heat generated while charging and discharging. Thus, a battery heat control system is needed. Liquid-cooled systems can effectively regulate the LIB pack's temperature in both charge and discharge situations. The utilization of fly ash nanoparticles distributed in water-as a coolant in indirect liquid cooling systems is the primary objective of the current work. The fly ash nanoparticle concentration and the BTMS capacity to remove heat were found to be clearly correlated in the experimental findings of the fly ash-based nanofluid (NF). In comparison to the base fluid, the fly ash water-based NF was able to remove around 10.65% more heat at a concentration of 0.1% by volume of nanoparticles, and approximately 11.30% more heat at a concentration of 0.3% and 13.04% more heat at a concentration of 1% volume fraction of fly ash-based nanoparticles. Increased thermal conductivity and better convective heat transfer properties of the NF with a greater fly ash concentration are responsible for this improvement in thermal performance. Comparing these nanofluids to traditional coolants, experiments have shown that they can lower thermal resistance and increase heat transfer coefficients. According to the study's findings, fly ash-based NF perform better at cooling than traditional coolants like ethylene glycol and water.

ce:section.ce:para: Two issues that have grown increasingly pressing in recent years are global warming and energy scarcity. In an effort to become carbon neutral by 2050, several nations aggressively encourage energy saving and emissions reduction [1]. The switch to electric vehicles (EVs) has significantly reduced life-cycle greenhouse gas emissions by 89%, making battery EVs the greenest alternative to traditional cars [2,3]. Due to their high energy density, long cycle life, and relatively low self-discharge rate, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have emerged as the most popular energy source for electric vehicles. LIBs are preferred over other power sources on the basis of performance, safety, recycling, green technology, battery lifespan, and aging, making them the most efficient and secure storage system [4,5].

LIBs have the potential to open the door to a more sustainable future as an alternative to energy generation based on fossil fuels. It is important to remember that Li-ion cells can only withstand a certain amount of heat; they can function safely between 15 and 35°C [6–12,50]. The Battery Thermal Management System (BTMS) tracks and reduces temperature rises inside the battery to address temperature-related issues in LIBs. An efficient BTMS is essential for preserving the battery pack's temperature balance and halting performance deterioration brought on by temperature-related problems. This greatly improves LIBs' performance and cycle life by guaranteeing that they always run within a safe and optimal temperature range [13]. The 3 main categories of BTMSs are liquid cooling systems, phase-change material (PCM) cooling systems, and air cooling systems [10,14–16]. Due to its many advantages, such as its simple design, low running costs, and ease of maintenance, air cooling is widely used. The limited specific heat capacity and poor thermal conductivity of air cooling, however, make it suitable mainly for applications that need little heat dissipation. Air cooling is unable to satisfy the thermal dissipation requirements under severe running conditions and runs the risk of causing a hazardous thermal runaway in the battery module [17].

Through heat absorption and phase change processes, PCM cooling can maintain exceptional temperature uniformity in the battery module, thanks to the remarkable benefit of high latent heat. Other advantages of this passive cooling technique are its affordability and compact size. However, the issue with PCM cooling systems is that, particularly in extreme operating conditions, their limited thermal conductivity prevents them from dissipating heat efficiently. The addition of metal, carbon, or their oxide components is one possible way to increase the thermal conductivity of PCMs. As the most widely marketed approach of BTMS [18], liquid-based BTMS can deliver more effective cooling performance. Furthermore, a cold plate is typically selected for liquid-based BTMS due to the strict space limitations and the danger of leakage [8]. There are 2 types of liquid cooling technologies: direct and indirect.

A direct liquid cooling system, also known as an immersion cooling system, immerses the battery cells in a dielectric coolant medium to enhance thermal resistance, temperature uniformity, and cooling efficiency. This is because the coolant comes into direct contact with every surface of the battery cells. Indirect liquid cooling uses cooling jackets, cooling plates, or cooling tubes to transfer heat to the battery module. The coolant in an indirect cooling system is typically either water or a water-ethylene glycol mixture. The temperature uniformity and cooling performance of the battery module are significantly enhanced by indirect liquid cooling, which has a larger specific heat capacity and thermal conductivity than air-cooled systems. However, the primary barrier to indirect cooling is the large thermal resistance between the coolant and the battery module, which substantially reduces the efficiency of heat dissipation. Chen et al. [18] verified that the coolant flow route significantly affects the liquid-based BTMS's heat transfer capability. As a result, many cold plates with optimal flow paths were suggested, including serpentine, vortex and zigzag types [19,20]. A more efficient cooling performance was also attained by optimizing the input velocity [21], length-to-width ratio of the flow route [22] and flow orientation [23]. According to Qian et al. [24], a cold plate with 5 mini-channels is sufficient to effectively maintain the battery pack's temperature. Deng et al. [25] used an orthogonal experimental design to improve the mini-channel cold plate's geometric characteristics. In order to better manage the flow resistance and thermal problems, Guo et al. [26,27] added pin fins to cold plates. Furthermore, improving the coolant's thermal conductivity is always a good technique to achieve better cooling results for liquid-based BTMS. As a result, several coolant types have already been created, including water, engine oil (EO) [28], ethylene glycol (EG) [29] and liquid metal [30]. The use of liquid cooling has been shown in several studies to enhance thermal control and postpone the start of thermal runaway. However, conventional liquid cooling techniques need to be improved much further to satisfy high-performance battery systems' rising thermal requirements. The cooling strategy based on nanofluids has drawn interest as a potential improvement over conventional liquid cooling techniques in response to this demand. Using this technique, thermal conductivity and heat transfer performance are improved by distributing nanoparticles into traditional base fluids. Particularly at high discharge rates, when batteries produce a lot of heat, nanofluids provide better heat dissipation. Integration into present battery designs is possible without significant changes since the base fluids used in nanofluids are often compatible with liquid cooling structures already in place. Because of this, nanofluids are a desirable and expandable choice for sophisticated battery heat control. The majority of research on nanofluid-based BTMS to far has relied on computer simulations because real experimental experiments are frequently expensive and time-consuming [31]. To get a more efficient cooling performance, nanofluid (NF), a combination made by suspending nanoparticles in the base fluid, was also added to BTMS in recent years [32].

Kanti et al. [33] have highlighted the synergistic interaction of SiO₂ and TiO₂ nanoparticles with graphene oxide in hybrid nanofluids, demonstrating enhanced thermal conductivity and viscosity stability across varying concentrations and temperatures. These hybrid formulations show promising potential as cost-effective, high-performance coolants for heat transfer applications above 45°C. Sarchami et al. [34] used water-Al2O3 to adjust the BTMS's geometric parameters and inlet velocity. A new BTMS for the 18650 type Li-ion battery was created by Tousi et al. [35] using water-AgO. The impact of Al2O3 NF on BTMS was also investigated by Liu et al. [28] who found that BTMS with Al2O3 NF is more successful in regulating thermal behavior but also suffers from a greater pressure loss. Author [36] explores the thermal behavior of fly ash–Cu hybrid nanofluids for solar energy systems, emphasizing their enhanced conductivity and viscosity across temperatures and concentrations. Advanced characterization and modeling techniques were employed to establish predictive correlations and assess heat transfer efficiency. By optimizing BTMS using TiO2 and Fe3O4 NFs, Wiriyasart et al. [37] were able to achieve the best cooling performance and pressure loss. Higher fluid inflow temperatures significantly raised the highest experienced temperature and temperature diversity during the discharge operation by roughly 6 and 5°C, respectively, according to experiments conducted by Wankhede et al. [38]. Kanti et al. [39] investigates convective heat transfer and friction factor performance of fly ash and fly ash–Cu hybrid nanofluids in a copper tube under uniform heat flux, revealing superior thermal properties at elevated temperatures. Experimental results highlight significant enhancements in Nusselt number and thermal performance factor, especially for hybrid nanofluids at higher concentrations. Kanti et al. [40] presents experimental and numerical analysis of forced convection and flow behavior of fly ash–Cu hybrid nanofluid under constant heat flux, revealing enhanced thermal performance with increasing concentration. The study establishes correlations for Nusselt number and friction factor, with computational results aligning closely with experimental findings. With the help of modeling and experimental studies, Kanti et al. [41] investigate how pH and mixing ratios affect the stability and thermophysical characteristics of CuO–GO hybrid nanofluids. BoA-SVM-based predictive models perform better than BoA-BRT for estimating viscosity and thermal conductivity at different temperatures and concentrations. Fly ash nanoparticle-based nanofluids in indirect cooling systems dramatically improve heat dissipation in EV battery packs, lowering peak temperatures and enhancing thermal uniformity, according to research by Wankhede et al. [42]. Simulations demonstrate that increasing flow velocity and nanoparticle concentration can improve heat removal by up to 84.14%, supporting effective and environmentally friendly energy solutions. According to studies by Boopathi et al. [43] and Tao et al. [44], fly ash nanofluids can perform better than traditional coolants in terms of heat transfer coefficient, exergy efficiency, and thermal conductivity when they are dissolved in ethylene glycol-water combinations. According to these results, fly ash nanofluids may prove to be effective substitutes for conventional automotive coolants, especially in heavy-duty or high-load applications.

All together, these research make it abundantly evident that cooling using nanofluids has become a viable strategy for improving BTMS performance. Conventional nanofluids simulated using Al2O3, SiO2, CuO, and TiO2 nanoparticles have been the focus of the majority of previous studies. Fly ash-based nanofluids exhibit higher thermal performance factors and better entropy management, especially at elevated temperatures [39].

It has already been shown that adding NFs to BTMS is a desirable way to improve the cooling efficiency of BTMS. To further increase the efficiency of liquid-cooled thermal management systems for LIBs, additional work appears to be required despite all of the aforementioned achievements, especially in the area of thermal conductivity enhancement employing NFs. The impact of fly ash-based NFs on the BTMS cooling performance using modeling tools has not received much attention. The application of fly ash-based nanoparticles in real BTMS has not been experimentally studied. In this study, experiments were conducted to close the aforementioned gaps.

In the current scenario, for the cooling of the battery pack, different NFs are used by the industries including Al2O3, CuO, TiO2, etc. These oxides are very expensive. Fly ash (FA) is a byproduct of power plants and a ceramic material. Good thermophysical characteristics and stability in the presence of surfactant are exhibited by nanosize FA particles in base liquid water. FA has a lower TC than metal NP, although its amorphous composition is influenced by various metal oxides. On the other hand, the amount of fly ash waste generated by the thermal power plant in India is very high around 169 million tons per annum which causes so much pollution. This research aims to solve this problem by using fly ash as a NF. The various chemical constituents of fly ash are listed in Table 1.

Lithium-ion NMC (Nickel Manganese Cobalt) 18650 cells are widely chosen for battery packs due to their superior performance characteristics, compact size, and energy density. These cylindrical cells, with dimensions of 18 mm in diameter and 65 mm in length, offer a high energy density of 150–220 Wh/kg, making them ideal for applications requiring lightweight and space-efficient energy storage, such as EVs, medical devices, and consumer electronics. Their nominal voltage of 3.7 V means that fewer cells are needed in series to achieve higher pack voltages, reducing system complexity and cost.

NMC cells are recognized for their balanced performance in terms of energy and power density, offering both high capacity and the ability to handle significant discharge rates. This hybrid performance characteristic makes them a preferred choice for applications needing consistent energy output and fast charging capabilities. Despite a shorter cycle life (approximately 1000–1500 cycles) compared to alternatives like LFP (Lithium Iron Phosphate) batteries, the higher energy density and smaller size of 18650 NMC cells remain advantageous for use in EVs and other portable devices where weight and volume constraints are critical. NMC technology has been a key driver in the adoption of lithium-ion batteries for EVs, with automakers such as BMW and Nissan utilizing these cells for their high-performance and mid-range electric vehicles.

A comparison of the performance characteristics of various commercially available lithium-ion cells is provided in Table 2. Based on this survey, 18650 NMC lithium-ion cells have been selected for the battery pack. Due to its compact size and high specific energy, it is easy to design a compact battery pack, as a result, many automobile companies use 18650 cells in their EVs. The specifications of the 18650 cylindrical cell are shown in Table 3.
This section focuses on the design of the battery pack, including the calculation of cell configuration and the arrangement of the cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: No. of cell and their arrangement

ce:list-item.ce:para:
No of cell in series (S) =
Desired voltage
Cell nominal voltage

No of cell in parallel (P) =
Desired capacity
Cell capacity

ce:section.ce:para: Battery pack configuration: 13S4P = 52 cells.

In this setup, a fly-ash-based NF is passed through a copper tube with an inner diameter of 6 mm and a wall thickness of 0.5 mm. The choice of a copper tube is crucial due to its excellent thermal conductivity, which facilitates efficient heat transfer between the fluid and the surroundings. The thermophysical properties of the copper tube are listed in Table 4. The small diameter of the tube enhances the surface area-to-volume ratio, promoting better interaction between the NF and the tube walls, thus optimizing heat exchange. The 0.5 mm wall thickness ensures structural integrity while minimizing thermal resistance, allowing the copper's high conductivity to dominate the heat transfer process. In this experimental study we analyse the thermal and flow characteristics of NFs in compact battery pack.

Sonication is a widely used technique to prepare NFs, including fly ash-based NFs. It involves dispersing nanoparticles into a base fluid (e.g., water, ethylene glycol) using ultrasonic energy. Below is a detailed procedure outlining the sonication process for fly ash-based NFs. The schematic of probe sonication is shown in Fig. 1, and the actual probe sonicator is depicted in Fig. 2.

The process of preparing nanoparticles of a given size using the probe sonication method begins with nanoparticle preparation. In the case of raw fly ash, ball milling or chemical modification may be employed to reduce particle size and improve surface properties. Next, the base fluid is prepared in the required quantity, and if surfactants are necessary, they are added at appropriate concentrations to enhance dispersion. During the pre-mixing stage, the required amount of fly ash nanoparticles is measured on a balance and gradually added to the base fluid using either manual stirring or a magnetic stirrer, to prevent the formation of clusters. Sonication begins by pouring the pre-mixed suspension into a sterilized beaker, which is then placed in an ice bath or cooling system to manage the heat generated during sonication. The ultrasonic probe is inserted into the suspension, ensuring it does not touch the container walls or bottom. Sonication parameters are then set, typically involving a frequency range of 20–40 kHz, power adjusted according to the fluid volume and nanoparticle concentration, and a duration of 20–60 min, depending on the desired level of dispersion. The sonicator is activated, and intermittent manual agitation is carried out to prevent sedimentation. During the process, the suspension’s temperature is monitored to ensure it remains below the stability threshold of both the base fluid and the nanoparticles, and dispersion is visually or analytically checked for homogeneity. After sonication, the suspension is cooled and, if necessary, filtered to remove large agglomerates. Finally, the NF is stored in a clean, airtight container to prevent contamination or settling. Throughout the procedure, equipment is monitored to avoid overheating or damage. By following these steps, a stable and homogeneous fly ash-based NF can be prepared for applications such as heat transfer enhancement or other industrial processes.

The AMS German brand Li-ion 18650 battery has a nominal capacity of 2500 mAh and uses Lithium Nickel Manganese Cobalt Oxide (NMC) chemistry. It operates at a nominal voltage of 3.6 V, with a charging voltage of 4.25 V and a discharge cut-off voltage of 2.5 V. The battery supports standard discharge at 2550 mAh and rapid discharge at 2400 mAh, with energy density rated at 185 Wh/kg. It has a cycle life of over 800 cycles, retaining more than 2000 mAh capacity, and operates in temperatures from 0 to 45°C for charging and −20 to 60°C for discharging. Dimensions are 65 mm in length and 18.4 mm in diameter, with a weight of 50 g. A battery pack with 13S4P configuration is as shown in Fig. 3. Charging is done using the constant current–constant voltage (CC–CV) method, and storage in a cool, dry place a ensures shelf life of up to 10 years. A Battery management system is recommended for safety, providing protections like over-voltage and short-circuit prevention. The battery passed rigorous tests (e.g., vibration, drop, and high temperature) to meet UL1642, GB 31241, and UN38.3 standards. It is ideal for applications requiring durability, safety, and high performance.

Fig. 4 shows the schematic of the experimental setup, which includes a 48 V, 15 Ah lithium-ion battery pack (BP), a 12 V radiator pump, a storage tank, connecting valves, tubing, and copper cooling tubes arranged around the BP in a serpentine pattern. The setup also includes digital temperature sensors, a discharge unit, and a thermocouple base. The heat exchange component—specifically the cooling pipe used for cylindrical batteries—is positioned on the side of the battery pack, as the side surface area is significantly greater than that of the bottom section, enhancing heat dissipation. Cylindrical batteries are widely used by automakers due to their superior efficiency, reliability, safety, and longevity compared to prismatic and pouch cells. Coolant circulates through the circuit to cool the battery pack when the pump is turned on. After absorbing heat from the BP, the coolant then goes back to the storage tank. This process is repeated until the BP temperature drops to the ideal processing temperature range for LIBs. Fig. 5 shows the actual experimental setup used for this study.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The NF in the storage tank is kept at a consistent temperature.
The pump has a constant flow rate.
The radiator provides a constant cooling effect.
The cooling fluid within the cooling tubes is not contaminated.
The ambient temperature is presumed to be constant.

ce:section.ce:para: The aim of this experiment is to compare the cooling capacities of several coolant types that will be used to keep the BP cool in real-world operating situations. The pump operates at 12 V DC with a current rating of 2 A, making it suitable for a variety of fluid transfer applications, including the circulation of NFs. It is designed for non-submersible use, often employed in small-scale fluid handling systems where portability and efficiency are key. The pump is typically constructed from durable, corrosion-resistant materials, ensuring longevity even when handling fluids like NFs. With a moderate flow rate, it provides reliable performance for controlled fluid movement and is ideal for systems where space and power constraints are present. This type of pump is commonly found in cooling systems, laboratory setups, and small industrial applications.

Digatron software is a powerful and versatile tool designed specifically for managing and analyzing battery testing processes, such as charging, discharging, and cycling. It works in tandem with battery testing equipment to provide real-time data monitoring and recording, ensuring accurate and reliable performance evaluation. The software supports the creation of customizable test profiles, allowing users to define specific parameters like voltage, current, and duration for various battery types and testing scenarios. With its advanced data acquisition capabilities, Digatron software logs critical parameters such as voltage, current, capacity, and temperature, enabling users to monitor battery behavior in real-time and store data for post-test analysis. The software also features intuitive visualization tools, including graphs and tables, to help interpret trends and performance metrics. Additionally, it supports automated report generation for documenting test results comprehensively. Designed with a user-friendly interface, Digatron software simplifies complex testing workflows while ensuring high precision and repeatability. It is widely used in research labs, manufacturing facilities, and quality control environments to optimize battery performance and reliability under diverse conditions.

Repeatability and systematic error seem to have been the 2 primary problems that plagued the experimental studies at all times. Table 5 lists the systematic instrumental errors that are a component of the experimental setup. However, as seen in Table 6, repeatability issues arise when results vary under constant experimental, hydraulic, thermal, and ambient conditions. To minimize this type of error in the experimental data, each experiment was conducted 5 times. The accuracy of the experimental data was assessed using the uncertainty analysis method proposed by Moffat [46]. The average uncertainties for all device data, such as coolant inlet/outlet pressure, temperature, and flow rate, were then determined using this calculation.
(1)
δ R =
∑
I = 1
N
δ R
δ Xi
×
δ Xi
2
2
2

In the above Eq. (1) [46], 'N' is the number of experiments conducted, while ∂R denotes uncertainty. The average uncertainty, measured value, and the deviation between the measured value and the average of measured values are represented by the expressions ∂R/∂Xi, δXi, and their corresponding parameters, respectively. The repeatability of key measuring devices is presented methodically in Tables 5 and 6. Interestingly, in this experimental setting, the only factor affecting the uncertainty estimates was the accuracy of each measuring instrument. To achieve this assessment, several variables were carefully measured, including temperature, voltage, current, volume flow rate, and so on. Error bar is as shown in Fig. 6.

Disparities in the assessment of thermal performance may also arise due to measurement errors in temperature sensors, flow rate controllers, and data acquisition systems. Additionally, the thermal responses can vary as a result of fluctuations in battery heat generation, caused by irregular load profiles or environmental factors. Unreliable or non-reproducible results can also be caused by improper equipment calibration and a lack of consistency in experimental protocols. To guarantee the validity and accuracy of experimental results, such errors must be carefully identified and minimized.

The experiment was conducted in the setup discussed earlier in the experimental setup section. At a nanoparticle concentration of 0.1% by volume, the NF was able to remove approximately 18% more heat compared to the base fluid, while a concentration of 0.3% resulted in a heat removal improvement of nearly 32%. This enhancement in thermal performance is attributed to the increased thermal conductivity and improved convective heat transfer characteristics of the NF with higher fly ash content.
Scenario 1: Rate of heat absorption for different coolants

Temperature at intake has been taken as 20 and testing is performed using each of the fluids listed in Table 7. Only the NF at 1 vol% concentration with 0.029 kg/s is used for the experimental study for 60 min. NFs comprising Al2O3, CuO and fly ash have been chosen as a coolant to be utilized in BTMS to compare their thermal conductivity. From Fig. 7 it is clear that fly ash-based NF is comparable with Al2O3 and superior as compared to other coolants.
Scenario 2: Impact of mass flow rate on cooling performance

The coolant flow velocity is another important factor influencing the BTM's cooling performance. As shown in Table 8, an increase in the coolant mass flow rate leads to a significant reduction in the battery pack temperature over time. The increased flow rate efficiently cools the BP, enhancing heat transfer from the BP to the coolant. For instance, at a mass flow rate of 0.5810 kg/s, the battery pack reaches the lowest temperature of 27°C at 60 s, compared to 30.5°C at the lowest flow rate of 0.0290 kg/s. The battery cooling system's performance is shown to be promising in Fig. 8. However, raising the coolant flow rate can enhance the cooling efficiency of liquid-cooled pipes. The coolant flow velocity is another important factor influencing the BTM's cooling performance.
Scenario 3: Effect of increase in maximum temperature of battery pack

The thermal performance of a battery pack cooled with a 1 vol% fly ash–water NF with mass flow rate of 0.029 kg/s was examined in the current experimental investigation. Although 40°C was originally established as the baseline maximum temperature, actual operating conditions may result in higher thermal loads. In order to test this, the battery pack's maximum temperature was varied from 40 to 70°C in 5°C increments (40, 45, 50, 55, 60, 65, and 70°C) while keeping the base case's intake and outlet flow conditions constant. Over a period of 10–60 s, the highest temperatures were noted. In all test situations, a general downward trend in peak temperature over time was noted, as illustrated in Fig. 9. The findings show that even at high operating temperatures, the 1 vol% fly ash nanofluid has comparatively better thermal management and efficient heat dissipation properties.
Scenario 4: Effect of volume fraction of fly ash-based NF

The volume percentage of the NF, which has been the focus of scholarly research and discussion, is one of the primary factors that determine thermal conductivity. The efficiency of fly ash-based NFs in controlling the surface temperature of battery cells over 20 min was assessed by methodically evaluating their cooling ability at various concentrations (0.1%, 0.3%, 0.5%, and 1% by volume). Cell Nos. 1, 13, 26, 39, and 52 had baseline cell temperatures of 44.6, 44.8, 45.0, 45.3, and 46.0°C, respectively, without any cooling. The relative cooling impact attained with each NF concentration was assessed using these data. The drop in temperature was negligible at a concentration of 0.1 vol% ( Fig. 10). The temperatures of Cell Nos. 1, 13, 26, 39, and 52 dropped to 43.0, 42.6, 43.4, 42.5, and 41.1°C, respectively, after 20 min of cooling. This indicates that, on average, the cells have cooled by about 2.6°C from their starting temperatures. Even though the temperature dropped noticeably, the performance was not very good, which shows that the thermal conductivity did not improve much at this low nanoparticle loading.

The thermal performance considerably improved when the concentration was raised to 0.3 vol% ( Fig. 11). The same cells' temperatures decreased to 42.7, 42.0, 43.0, 42.1, and 40.8°C, respectively, after 20 min. Cell No. 52 exhibits a 5.2°C decrease from its baseline value, indicating further improvement in cooling capability. This suggests that heat dissipation becomes more efficient as nanoparticle concentration rises. The observed temperature decrease is a result of greater fluid convection and increased thermal conductivity at this concentration. The nanofluid demonstrated significantly higher thermal performance at a concentration of 0.5 vol% ( Fig. 12). The temperatures of Cell Nos. 1, 13, 26, 39, and 52 dropped to 41.2, 42.0, 42.8, 42.0, and 43.0°C, respectively, after 20 min. Notably, Cell No. 26 displayed a decrease of 2.2°C, whilst Cell No. 1 displayed a cooling of 3.4°C. Due to improved nanoparticle dispersion and decreased thermal resistance, Cell No. 52 showed a lesser temperature drop than at 0.3 vol%, but the overall average cooling effect stayed constant. Fly ash NF at 1% concentration produced the best cooling results ( Fig. 13). After 20 min, the 5 cells' respective temperatures decreased to 40.0, 41.1, 41.6, 41.1, and 40.1°C. This corresponds to average drops in temperature from baseline of 4.6−5.9°C for all cells. The greatest cooling occurred in Cell No. 52, which dropped 5.9°C from 46.0 to 40.1°C. This is explained by the nanofluid's enhanced thermal conductivity at greater particle loadings, which facilitates more effective heat transfer between the cooling medium and the cell surfaces.

Overall, the findings show a distinct pattern of improved cooling efficiency as the quantity of nanoparticles increases. The greater thermal conductivity of the nanofluid, improved dispersion of nanoparticles, and increased surface area for heat exchange are the main causes of the improved thermal performance. Though the greatest temperature reductions were obtained at 1 vol% concentration, practical applications should take into account possible disadvantages such increased viscosity and the possibility of agglomeration at larger particle loadings. These results imply that fly ash-based nanofluids are potential options for battery thermal control in high-performance energy storage systems, especially at concentrations of 0.5 vol% and 1 vol%.

The potential increase in dynamic viscosity, which may lead to higher pressure drops and consequently increased pumping power requirements, represents a critical practical concern when using fly ash-based NF for battery cooling. Experimental studies by Kanti et al. [47] indicate that at low nanoparticle concentrations (≤0.5 wt%), the viscosity of fly ash-based NFs increases moderately, typically between 5 and 15% compared to the base fluid. Our calculations suggest that the notable enhancement in thermal conductivity compensates for this slight increase in viscosity, resulting in a favorable heat transfer-to-viscosity ratio. Nonetheless, factors such as nanoparticle shape, flow rate, and dispersion quality influence pressure drop and are highly system-dependent.

While higher volume fractions of fly ash-based nanofluids enhance cooling performance, they can also lead to nanoparticle agglomeration and elevated flow resistance. To mitigate these issues, the dispersion stability was improved through surfactant selection (e.g., Polyvinylpyrrolidone, Sodium dodecyl sulfate) and ultrasonication trials, with optimal sonication times identified in the range of 90–150 min. pH tuning and surface functionalization of fly ash particles further reduced aggregation risks. Additionally, viscosity management was achieved by optimizing concentration below 1 vol%, and flow resistance was controlled through hybrid formulations and geometric design considerations in heat exchange systems.

Furthermore, when considering the use of fly ash-based nanofluids in EV battery cooling systems, it is essential to evaluate their impact on battery pack integrity, material corrosion, and electrical insulation:
•
Battery Pack Integrity
Fly ash nanoparticles—primarily composed of silica, alumina, and iron oxides—may settle or agglomerate over time if not adequately stabilized, potentially causing clogging or uneven cooling. Additionally, particle interactions with components such as gaskets, seals, or composite housings could degrade elastomers or polymers. The use of surfactants or surface modification of nanoparticles can enhance dispersion stability and mitigate these risks.
•
Material Corrosion
Battery cooling systems often employ aluminum, copper, and various alloys that are sensitive to pH fluctuations and ionic contamination. Fly ash may introduce trace metals or alkaline components, potentially accelerating galvanic or pitting corrosion, especially in aluminum heat exchangers or coolant channels. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and weight loss tests under dynamic conditions can quantify corrosion rates. Assessments under simulated EV operating conditions (thermal cycling, vibration, electrical exposure) are recommended. Mitigation strategies include maintaining neutral or controlled pH, using corrosion inhibitors compatible with both nanofluid chemistry and system materials, and applying protective surface coatings.
•
Electrical Insulation
Given the presence of high-voltage components in EV battery packs, the dielectric properties of the coolant are critical. Fly ash particles can affect the electrical conductivity of the base fluid depending on their composition, concentration, and surface charge. Important parameters include dielectric breakdown voltage, which must remain sufficiently high to prevent arcing or short circuits, as well as volume resistivity and permittivity, which must stay within safe limits for high-voltage applications. To address these challenges, nanoparticle concentration should be kept within ranges that do not compromise dielectric properties, nanoparticles can be encapsulated or surface-treated to reduce conductivity, and hybrid nanofluids combining fly ash with insulating oxides such as SiO2 may be developed to balance thermal and electrical performance.

This article uses fly ash water-based NFs as a coolant instead of pure fluid to quantitatively investigate a BP of 48 V under constant wall temperature. Fly ash water-based NFs have been employed as a coolant for battery cooling at 4 different concentrations. The results of the current investigation allow for the following observations to be made:
•
Fly ash nanofluid, at a 1 vol% concentration, demonstrated the highest thermal performance, achieving a reduction of up to 5.9°C in critical cell temperatures over a 20-min period, surpassing the performance of Al2O3 and CuO nanofluids.
•
The cooling efficiency was directly influenced by the mass flow rate. At the maximum tested rate of 0.5810 kg/s, the temperature of the battery pack decreased to 27°C after 60 s, in contrast to 30.5°C observed at the minimum flow rate of 0.029 kg/s.
•
The management of elevated maximum battery pack temperatures (40−70°C) was successfully achieved with the use of 1 vol% fly ash nanofluid, showing consistent trends in temperature reduction over time and confirming its reliability in real-world operating conditions.
•
The increasing of nanoparticle concentration from 0.1 to 1 vol% enhanced the average temperature reduction from approximately 2.6 to 4.36°C, attributed to better thermal conductivity and convective heat transfer. Though the greatest temperature reductions were obtained at 1 vol% concentration.

Overall, fly ash-based nanofluids exhibit superior heat transfer performance compared to water and offer a cost-effective alternative to conventional coolants for BTMS applications, particularly in scenarios demanding enhanced thermal efficiency.

As the demand for EVs and high-performance energy storage systems continues to rise, the development of efficient and sustainable cooling solutions for BTMS is critical. The utilization of fly ash-based NFs as a cooling medium presents a unique opportunity to tackle challenges such as excessive heat generation and uneven temperature distribution in battery packs. This novel approach not only enhances the thermal management efficiency of the system but also facilitates the sustainable reutilization of industrial by-products such as fly ash. With this technology advancing, numerous areas for further research and practical application become evident, making it relevant in both scientific research and industrial domains. The use of fly ash water-based NFs as the coolant in BTMS for electric vehicles EVs has a lot of promise to improve performance and efficiency. Some potential future applications include:
•
Blending fly ash-based NFs with PCMs is another promising area. It leverages the latent heat storage capacity of PCMs to ensure stable temperature control during peak thermal loads, thereby enhancing system performance and stability.
•
Fly ash-based NFs possess higher thermal conductivity than conventional coolants, which enhances heat dissipation in battery packs. This could result in improved and longer-lasting battery performance.
•
Fly ash NFs can be tailored to meet the specific cooling requirements of different EV models, from buses and trucks to smaller cars.
•
By reducing the environmental impact of battery production and disposal, using NFs might help EVs become more sustainable overall by improving their energy efficiency and minimizing the need for frequent battery changes.
•
The enhanced cooling capacity of fly ash NFs can lower the energy consumption of the thermal management system thereby increasing the overall efficiency of the EV by reducing the load on the battery for cooling.
•
Better production and stabilization methods will increase the cost-effectiveness of fly ash NFs, making them a more appealing option for broad application in EVs.
•
Collaboration between materials scientists, thermal engineers, and automakers can drive innovations in fly ash NFs-based BTMS, potentially leading to major breakthroughs in EV technology.
•
Increased cooling capacity can reduce the energy consumption of the thermal management system. This can increase the overall efficiency of the EV by reducing the demand on the battery for cooling.
•
All these research directions align with global targets are consistent with worldwide efficiency and sustainability targets, and they provide new avenues for utilizing industrial wastes in high-technology applications. Through sustained development, NFs derived from fly ash might have a revolutionary contribution to contemporary thermal management technologies.
•
Future research may aim at developing next-generation flow channels, like helical or stair-step-patterned geometries, to maximize heat transfer without excessive pressure drops, making the battery cooling system more efficient overall.
•
In addition, studies must aim to scale up the manufacture of fly ash-based NFs and incorporate them into industrial applications.
By addressing these problems and creating thermal management systems for electric automobiles, fly ash-based NFs can greatly enhance their efficiency, economics, and sustainability.
The authors declare that they have not received any financial support for the conduct of this research.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support and research facilities provided by Pimpri Chinchwad College of Engineering, Pune.
